.review/results/otp-5b-source-responder-data-plane.codex.md:10943:- `crates/blit-core/src/transfer_session/mod.rs:1735` — **Medium** — If a DESTINATION initiator receives `accept.data_plane` but `data_plane_host` is `None`, it silently takes the in-stream branch. The SOURCE responder has already granted TCP and blocks in `accept_source_data_plane()` waiting for that dial, so this cannot actually fall back; it degrades into a bounded 30s accept timeout instead of fail-fast. This violates the contract that the initiator dials when a grant exists. Mirror `source_send_half`’s explicit “grant but no host” error.
.review/results/otp-5b-source-responder-data-plane.codex.md:10945:VERDICT: FAIL — one ordering/fallback bug found; push path and single-stream resize suppression otherwise look unchanged/correct from the diff.
.review/results/otp-5b-source-responder-data-plane.codex.md:10948:- `crates/blit-core/src/transfer_session/mod.rs:1735` — **Medium** — If a DESTINATION initiator receives `accept.data_plane` but `data_plane_host` is `None`, it silently takes the in-stream branch. The SOURCE responder has already granted TCP and blocks in `accept_source_data_plane()` waiting for that dial, so this cannot actually fall back; it degrades into a bounded 30s accept timeout instead of fail-fast. This violates the contract that the initiator dials when a grant exists. Mirror `source_send_half`’s explicit “grant but no host” error.
.review/results/otp-5b-source-responder-data-plane.codex.md:10950:VERDICT: FAIL — one ordering/fallback bug found; push path and single-stream resize suppression otherwise look unchanged/correct from the diff.

docs/bugs/copy-destination-semantics.md:3:**Status:** **RESOLVED.** Fixed by adopting rsync's exact trailing-slash algorithm (`flist.c:send_file_list` DOTDIR_NAME + `main.c:get_local_name`) uniformly across all four transport combinations. Resolution lives at `crates/blit-app/src/transfers/resolution.rs::resolve_destination` (Phase 5 A.0 move; the CLI's `crates/blit-cli/src/transfers/mod.rs` keeps a `pub(crate) use` re-export so the dispatch site reads the same as before). Local→local, local→remote, remote→local, and remote→remote all see the same resolved destination. Regression tests pin each rule and case: `crates/blit-cli/tests/single_file_copy.rs`, `crates/blit-cli/tests/remote_pull_subpath.rs`, `crates/blit-cli/tests/remote_move.rs`, and the unit tests in `crates/blit-app/src/transfers/resolution.rs`. Manpage section "Destination Semantics (rsync-style)" in `docs/cli/blit.1.md` documents the full table; `blit copy --help` now includes the PATHS summary inline.
docs/bugs/copy-destination-semantics.md:140:- Parse the raw CLI string before it becomes a `PathBuf` (since `PathBuf` strips trailing slashes on some platforms). Record a `source_is_dir_contents: bool` flag on `TransferArgs`.
docs/bugs/copy-destination-semantics.md:141:- In the orchestrator, if `!source_is_dir_contents && src.is_dir()`, rewrite `dest_root = dest_root.join(src.file_name())` before planning.
docs/bugs/copy-destination-semantics.md:171:- **Windows paths:** Verify trailing-slash parsing works for `C:\path\` as well as `C:/path/`. The existing endpoint parser in `crates/blit-core/src/remote/endpoint.rs` already normalizes separators for remote paths; local paths need the same treatment *before* `PathBuf` canonicalization eats the trailing slash.

.review/findings/audit-15-grpc-missing-connection-timeouts.md:27:Configure a default request/connection timeout on the gRPC server using `Server::builder().timeout(...)`. This limits the maximum duration any single RPC request is allowed to take before being aborted by the server. 
.review/findings/audit-15-grpc-missing-connection-timeouts.md:111:Verified and Accepted. The coding team's pushback is correct. Applying a global `Server::timeout(30s)` is harmful as it would abruptly abort the 7 long-running control plane and data streaming RPCs (such as `Subscribe`, `DelegatedPull`, `Pull`, `PullSync`, `Push`, `Find`, and `DiskUsage`).

docs/DECISIONS.md:47:- Decision: `docs/STATE.md` is the single entry point for current state, with the precedence order in `AGENTS.md` §1; DEVLOG.md is write-only history, TODO.md is backlog-only, tool-local memories are scratch.
docs/DECISIONS.md:58:- Decision: the adaptive-streams stack (live-progress → PR1 telemetry → PR2 work-queue → PR2 review fix, up to `eafb187`) lands later as a planned `docs/plan/` slice via cherry-pick or rebase onto fresh commits — never via `git merge` of the branch (see D-2026-06-07-1 trap). `d9d4ec7` (PR3 WIP, "DOES NOT BUILD") is explicitly excluded until it is finished and compiles.
docs/DECISIONS.md:68:- Decision: All Phase C slices (`AUDIT_REPORT_2026-06-11_DESIGN.md`) ratified as proposed and entered into REVIEW.md in the proposed order. Embedded decisions: (a) **W2.4** — the deprecated Pull RPC is deleted once W2.3 has harvested its multi-stream pattern; criterion applied: not needed for FAST/SIMPLE/RELIABLE in any scenario. (b) **W8.1** — `zero_copy.rs` is **excluded** from the dead-code deletion sweep; owner judges it has FAST potential; disposition is an evaluation slice (`w8-1b`) that either produces a plan doc to wire splice into the receive pipeline or concludes deletion. (c) **W2.3** — writing the multi-stream-pull plan doc is authorized (no code before Status: Active).
docs/DECISIONS.md:74:- Why: the dead draft busy-waits on EAGAIN (would be rewritten, not revived); wiring needs a raw-fd special case beside a permanent buffered fallback; the CPU saving is a fraction of one core, Linux-only, and unmeasured. Revisit gate: 10 GbE benchmarks showing receive-side CPU saturation — design notes preserved in the eval doc.
docs/DECISIONS.md:78:- Decision: The 2026-06-14 "redesign the transfer subsystem from the ground up" framing is resolved as **convergence**, not a rebuild. One src/dst-agnostic sequencer owns all four paths (local↔local, push, pull, daemon↔daemon); the dial (stream count + all transfer knobs) is a single live object adjusted from measured telemetry; the already-shared byte-moving leaf stays. Dials are **bounded-unilateral** (receiver advertises a capacity ceiling; sender owns the dial within it) ~~and **size-gated** (small transfers skip the probe entirely)~~ **(size-gate framing superseded by D-2026-06-20-2 q1 — there is no probe phase to skip; the engine moves within ~1s and tunes live)**. The adaptive-streams stack (PR1 telemetry + PR2 work-stealing queue, up to `eafb187`) is salvaged as the substrate per D-2026-06-07-2; PR3 WIP (`d9d4ec7`) stays excluded. ~~Built A-first (warmup), C-ready by construction (mutable dial + elastic stream-set exist from A, so continuous adjustment is a later feed, not a retrofit).~~ **(A/warmup staging superseded by D-2026-06-20-2 q1 — conservative start + live tuning from the first byte; C shipped as `ue-r2-2` under REV4/D-2026-06-20-5.)** Plan: `docs/plan/UNIFIED_TRANSFER_ENGINE.md` (Draft — awaiting owner Draft→Active flip). *(Stale wording struck 2026-07-04 on owner direction — "follow the existing pattern": the in-place-annotation pattern of D-2026-06-20-3/-6. The convergence direction itself stands unchanged.)*
docs/DECISIONS.md:96:- Why: review found the Active plan's direction is sound but several slices need tightening before code starts: streaming initial planning was hidden inside `ue-1c`, local fast paths need to become engine-owned strategies, work-stealing is observable behavior, wire compatibility needs concrete shape, and pull parity gates must wait for multistream pull.
docs/DECISIONS.md:125:- Decision: `docs/plan/SMALL_FILE_CEILING.md` is **Active** (owner "go", 2026-07-04). sf-1 (tripwire harness) starts now; the in-plan gates stand unchanged — sf-6's wire-design owner sign-off before any code, and the sf-4/sf-7 acceptance reviews with the owner.
docs/DECISIONS.md:135:- Decision: Blit has NO version-compatibility obligation of any kind, in any direction, at any time — owner standing rule, restated with force 2026-07-05: "backward compatibility is NOT a consideration. I expect blit 1.2.3 not to be able to talk to blit-daemon 1.2.3.1. period. same build only. do not engineer tech debt into an unshipped product." Client and daemon interoperate only when built from the same source; the wire handshake must REFUSE a mismatched peer outright at session open (exact protocol/build identity — mechanism specified in ONE_TRANSFER_PATH otp-1 and pinned by test). Feature-capability bits that exist to tolerate version skew ("advisory until both peers advertise support", `supports_stream_resize`-style flags) are dead weight and go away with the unified session. NOT affected: the receiver capacity profile (runtime capacity of the receiving machine, D-2026-06-20-1/-2) — that is hardware negotiation, not version negotiation.
docs/DECISIONS.md:140:- Decision: The D-2026-06-12-1 revisit gate ("receive-side CPU saturation") is **declared met by the owner** (2026-07-05): a UniFi UNAS 8 Pro daemon target whose CPU cannot saturate 10 GbE even from SSD cache. Zero-copy receive is unparked as sanctioned FAST work. Two clarifications: (a) the dead `zero_copy.rs` module still gets deleted as ratified — its EAGAIN busy-wait draft is a rewrite, not a revival (eval doc); (b) the capability returns the one-path way (owner exchange 2026-07-05): a **runtime-selected write strategy inside the unified receive sink** — the eval doc's revisit design (`AsyncFd`-readiness splice loop beside the buffered relay, selected when the reader is a raw TcpStream and the payload is a file record, buffered relay as universal fallback), capability-gated by kernel/fs support, identical in both roles — never a side path. Sequenced after ONE_TRANSFER_PATH's cutover (otp-10) as its own slice set; the UNAS is the measurement rig and the symmetric-endpoint benchmark rule (D-2026-07-05-2 era methodology) applies to its cells.

.review/findings/otp-4b-data-plane.md:381:## Known gaps (carried)
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:17:- `cargo test --workspace` passes when run outside the sandbox with local port binding allowed: 185 tests passed, 1 doc test ignored.
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:23:The codebase has a solid core shape: the newer `TransferSource` / `TransferSink` / shared pipeline design is the right abstraction, local and remote transfer paths are converging, and the current test suite has broad integration coverage for admin verbs, remote push/pull, fallback, resume, remote-to-remote, and path semantics.
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:25:The main issues are boundary issues rather than architecture issues. Receive-side path handling is not centralized and still has unsafe joins. The daemon exposes `use_chroot` as a config/documented feature but appears not to enforce it. Filtered mirror delete semantics need an explicit product decision. The in-progress metrics and `check` features compile and test, but need lifecycle, endpoint, and semantics hardening before being treated as production-grade.
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:56:Move a single component-aware sanitizer into shared core code and use it before every receive-side filesystem join. It should reject absolute paths, `..`, Windows prefixes, root components, and platform-specific absolute-style strings. It should preserve the existing empty-path behavior only for single-file transfers where the destination root is already the final file path. Add tests for streamed files, tar shards, block writes, block completion, Unix absolute paths, Windows drive prefixes, UNC-style paths, and valid filenames containing literal `..`.
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:81:Either implement the promised boundary or remove/rename the option until it exists. If the intended model is "chroot-like containment" rather than OS `chroot(2)`, canonicalize the target and enforce `target.starts_with(module_root)` for operations that follow symlinks, with care for paths that do not exist yet during writes. For write paths, validate each existing parent component before creating the final file. Add symlink escape tests for list, pull, push, purge, find, du, and completions with `use_chroot = true`.
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:100:Consider defaulting to `127.0.0.1` and requiring `--bind 0.0.0.0` or config opt-in for LAN exposure. If LAN-by-default is intentional, emit a startup warning when binding a non-loopback address without an explicit acknowledgement. Keep the current docs, but make the runtime behavior harder to expose accidentally.
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:125:The new metrics code increments `active_transfers` before spawning transfer tasks and decrements it after the awaited handler returns:
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:133:If a spawned task panics or is aborted before reaching the decrement, the gauge stays elevated. The error counter also only increments when the handler returns an error status, not when the task panics. This can make Prometheus output misleading during exactly the failures operators need to diagnose.
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:139:Use an RAII guard that increments on construction and decrements on `Drop`, then move the guard into the spawned task. If panic accounting is required, wrap the handler or inspect join handles where practical. Decide whether operation counters mean "attempts" or "successes"; if attempts, increment purge before validation/deletion and add separate success/error counters. If successes, rename metrics accordingly.
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:141:### F6. Metrics HTTP server needs basic hardening before production use
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:152:For an opt-in internal endpoint this is acceptable as a starting point, but it has no read timeout, no connection concurrency limit, no method validation, and path matching accepts any path starting with `/metrics` or `/health`. If an operator binds this to a non-loopback interface, it is another unauthenticated endpoint to harden and document.
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:156:Add a read timeout, validate `GET`, enforce exact path or documented prefix behavior, and document that `--metrics-addr` should normally be loopback or behind a trusted scrape network. Add an async test that starts the endpoint, performs `/metrics`, `/health`, unsupported method, and not-found requests.
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:162:`RemoteTransferSource::prepare_payload` builds tar shards by reading each remote file into a per-file `Vec`, then appending it into a tar builder backed by another `Vec`:
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:165:- The TODO at `crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/source.rs:164` acknowledges the double-buffering.
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:173:Enforce a strict tar-shard byte budget in `RemoteTransferSource` before allocating, not only in the planner. Prefer streaming tar construction when practical, or fall back to individual file payloads when advertised headers exceed the safe tar-shard budget. Add tests with oversized header metadata from a fake source.
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:189:A single accepted tar shard can allocate 1 GiB before extraction. With multiple streams, that can exceed the host memory budget. The limits prevent unbounded `u64::MAX` allocations, but they are still high enough to create operational instability.
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:231:### F11. Pull checksum capability acknowledgement is discarded
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:245:Store the ack capability in pull state and use it to decide comparison behavior, progress messaging, and warnings. Add a test where daemon checksums are disabled and verify the client behavior is intentional.
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:284:### F14. Remaining warnings should be paid down before release
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:299:Prefix the unused test variable or assert the result. Track migration from `fsevent_sys::FSEventsGetCurrentEventId` to the recommended `objc2-core-services` path.
docs/reviews/codebase_review_2026-05-01.md:324:The main architecture gap is that path safety is not yet treated as a first-class shared abstraction. There are multiple sanitizers and multiple direct joins. This should be pulled up into a single tested module before adding more protocol features.

.review/findings/audit-14-resume-copy-redundant-seek.md:56:1. `src_file` is read sequentially. Its file pointer is always at `offset` before `read_exact`. Calling `src_file.seek(SeekFrom::Start(offset))` in every iteration is completely redundant.
.review/findings/audit-14-resume-copy-redundant-seek.md:57:2. `dst_file` is read sequentially (when checking hashes). Its file pointer is also advanced by `read_exact` to `offset + this_block`. Calling `dst_file.seek(SeekFrom::Start(offset))` before `read_exact` is redundant for every iteration except the first, or when a write occurred.
.review/findings/audit-14-resume-copy-redundant-seek.md:58:3. If a write occurs (`should_write` is true), we do need to seek the destination file back to `offset` because the previous `read_exact` advanced it to `offset + this_block`. After `write_all`, the destination file pointer is again at `offset + this_block`, which matches the next loop's starting offset.
.review/findings/audit-14-resume-copy-redundant-seek.md:64:Track the logical file pointer positions of the source and destination files (or keep track of whether they are already at the correct offset) and avoid issuing `seek` system calls unless necessary.
.review/findings/audit-14-resume-copy-redundant-seek.md:66:- For `dst_file`, keep track of `dst_cursor_pos`. Only seek if the current cursor position does not match the target `offset`.
.review/findings/audit-14-resume-copy-redundant-seek.md:101:            // Seek back to write offset (always needed after a read_exact advanced it, 
.review/findings/audit-14-resume-copy-redundant-seek.md:132:`dst_cursor_pos` tracker so the dst read-seek and write-seek fire only
.review/findings/audit-14-resume-copy-redundant-seek.md:143:mismatched block whose write must seek back over the read, then matched
.review/findings/audit-14-resume-copy-redundant-seek.md:149:Verified. Removing the redundant sequential source `seek` and tracking `dst_cursor_pos` to eliminate destination `seek` calls on aligned blocks eliminates unnecessary kernel transitions during block-level resume checks. Verification of correctness has been fully confirmed by existing integration tests (`test_resume_corrupted_block`, etc.) which cover matched, mismatched, and partial blocks.
.review/findings/audit-13-buffer-pool-double-locking.md:59:Acquire the `cache` lock only once. Furthermore, to avoid redundant zeroing, do not call `.clear()` before resizing. Instead, simply adjust the vector length using `.resize` or `.truncate` to restore its logical length, which will keep the existing (dirty) capacity intact without writing zeroes.
.review/findings/audit-13-buffer-pool-double-locking.md:108:**Safety verified before dropping the zeroing** (the one real risk — a
.review/findings/audit-13-buffer-pool-double-locking.md:116:a dirtied buffer is reused (not reallocated) and handed back at full
.review/findings/audit-12-buffer-pool-leak.md:42:dropped (releasing the semaphore) as the stack unwinds.

.review/findings/audit-11-data-plane-underflow.md:62:`remaining` before subtracting:
.review/findings/audit-11-data-plane-underflow.md:79:loopback TCP pair (the `DataPlaneSession` holds a concrete `TcpStream`, so
.review/findings/audit-10-cancel-completion-race.md:23:the `cancel_token.cancelled()` branch *before* it evaluates the `handler_ok`
.review/findings/audit-10-cancel-completion-race.md:37:completed before recording the outcome. A simple fix: if `handle_delegated_pull`
.review/findings/audit-10-cancel-completion-race.md:66:  transfer remains cancellable / hangup-abortable exactly as before.
.review/findings/audit-1-daemon-timeouts.md:10:network operations lack explicit `tokio::time::timeout` guards. These gaps are
.review/findings/audit-1-daemon-timeouts.md:12:(`feedback_server_await_timeouts.md` memory) that every `.await` on a socket
.review/findings/audit-1-daemon-timeouts.md:19:1. **`crates/blit-daemon/src/delegation_gate.rs:257-258`** — `lookup_host((host, port)).await` for DNS resolution in the SSRF gate. No timeout means a slow/blackhole DNS server stalls the `DelegatedPull` handler for OS-resolver timeout (5-30s).
.review/findings/audit-1-daemon-timeouts.md:27:5. **`crates/blit-daemon/src/service/delegated_pull.rs:176-179`** — DelegatedPull accepts TCP port 0 (IANA reserved). Should reject at the boundary with clear DelegationRejected error before proceeding to DNS + connect.
.review/findings/audit-1-daemon-timeouts.md:29:6. **`crates/blit-daemon/src/active_jobs.rs:759`** — `arm_persistence_at` uses `mpsc::unbounded_channel()` for persistence signals. Under disk-full + sustained completion rate, signals accumulate without backpressure. (Low severity — writer coalesces via `try_recv()` loop.)

docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:12:classifies errors that blit-core mints and strips before the classifier can see
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:26:daemon server but no client; readonly-clearing before delete exists on two of
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:37:   pull fallback loops, `jobs watch`, and the TUI forwarder forever. RELIABLE.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:81:   messages), but no verified path packs >4 MiB into one message today. Phase B
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:86:blit-core stacks **five strata without demolition** (sync/rayon local engine vs
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:117:by hand before filing. Three confirmed and filed as `[ ]` Open findings —
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:143:No module owns connection construction; ownership is smeared across five crates with one bright spot (mDNS, genuinely owned by crates/blit-core/src/mdns.rs). The gRPC control-plane channel is built three independent times with identical 30s+30s deadlines and an identical pasted audit comment (blit-core pull.rs:239, blit-core push/client/mod.rs:307, blit-app client.rs:37) — agreeing today, structurally guaranteed to diverge on the next fix, since blit-core cannot reuse blit-app's helper. The sharpest problem is direction asymmetry on the TCP data plane: push sockets get NODELAY + keepalive + auto-tuned buffers on both ends, while the entire pull/pull-sync direction (client connect at pull.rs:1710 and all three daemon accept paths) runs on raw kernel defaults, silently discarding the auto-tuner's tcp_buffer_size and leaving Nagle enabled — a direct FAST violation invisible to users. Both data-plane TcpStream::connect calls are also the only unbounded connects left in the codebase, reproducing the exact hang the audit-2 wave fixed for gRPC. Finally, the daemon's HTTP/2 keepalive (main.rs:138) has no client-side counterpart on any channel, and the workspace relies on tonic's unstated 4 MiB decode limit, guarded only by an undocumented 1 MiB fallback-chunk clamp.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:147:- Unbounded data-plane connects: both TCP data-plane client connects — push (crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/data_plane.rs:92) and pull (crates/blit-core/src/remote/pull.rs:1710) — call TcpStream::connect with no timeout, while every control-plane connect is bounded two or three times over. A firewalled/black-holed data port hangs the transfer for the OS SYN timeout (the very 60-127s failure mode the audit-2 comments document and fixed only for gRPC). RELIABLE violation: it does not fail plainly, it hangs.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:150:- Implicit 4 MiB tonic decode limit: no max_decoding_message_size/max_encoding_message_size anywhere in the workspace. Safety currently rests on GRPC_FALLBACK_CHUNK_BYTES=1 MiB (grpc_fallback.rs:102) clamping data frames, but that invariant is undocumented and unenforced for non-chunk messages (manifest batches, recent-runs state) — a growth in any message crosses the cliff with an opaque tonic error, not a plain Blit error.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:162:  - divergence: Push client socket: TCP_NODELAY (hard error, data_plane.rs:98-100), SO_KEEPALIVE best-effort (106-108), auto-tuned SO_SNDBUF/SO_RCVBUF (110-121). Pull client socket: bare TcpStream::connect — none of those options, ever. Both share one omission: NO connect timeout on TcpStream::connect (the control-plane connects are triple-bounded but neither data-plane connect is bounded at all; a black-holed data port hangs for the OS SYN timeout the audit comments elsewhere call out as 60-127s). Both directions do get a 30s stall guard, but only after the connection is established (data_plane.rs:68, pull.rs:1764).
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:180:- `tonic decode limit (implicit)` = ABSENT — rg for max_decoding_message_size/max_encoding_message_size over all crates returns zero hits; everyone inherits tonic's 4 MiB default (proto/blit.proto:1) — RELIABLE: data frames are clamped to 1 MiB (grpc_fallback.rs:102,110-112) so they fit, but nothing protects manifest-shaped messages or future growth; a >4 MiB message fails with tonic's opaque ResourceExhausted, not a plain Blit error.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:185:- `PlannedPayloads.chunk_bytes` = TCP-tuned chunk size (16-32 MiB) embedded in the transport-agnostic payload plan (crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/payload.rs:112) — FAST/SIMPLE: layering leak (seed example) — when the plan is consumed by gRPC fallback sinks it is clamped to 1 MiB at payload.rs:251, making the tuned value inert on that transport.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:187:- `GRPC_FALLBACK_CHUNK_BYTES` = 1 MiB (clamp at lines 110-112) (crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/grpc_fallback.rs:102) — RELIABLE: this clamp is the only thing keeping data frames under the implicit 4 MiB decode limit; the dependency is undocumented at either end.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:191:- `MAX_CONCURRENT_SCRAPES / listen backlog / bridge timeouts` = 64 permits; backlog 1024 (line 85); head 5s (39), scrape 8s (47), write 10s (53); ONESHOT_TIMEOUT 8s (main.rs:36) (crates/blit-prometheus-bridge/src/server.rs:63) — none — coherent local policy, well-commented; only note: the 8s scrape deadline silently overrides the shared 30s connect deadline, a third connect policy for the same daemon.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:210:- /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-daemon/src/main.rs:60 — mDNS advertise call site (daemon startup) — Failure to advertise degrades to a warning, not fatal (78-81).
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:212:- /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-prometheus-bridge/src/server.rs:69 — Bridge HTTP listener construction (build_listener) — TcpSocket + SO_REUSEADDR + backlog 1024; accept loop gated by Semaphore MAX_CONCURRENT_SCRAPES=64 (line 63); per-conn timeouts REQUEST_HEAD_TIMEOUT=5s (39), SCRAPE_TIMEOUT=8s (47), WRITE_TIMEOUT=10s (53).
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:216:- /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-tui/src/daemons.rs:335 — TUI site (recorded only): builds RemoteEndpoint from mDNS rows (127.0.0.1:9031 fallback at 335, addr:port at 338) — Feeds the shared connect_with_timeout path; not dug into further per scope.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:223:No single module owns timeouts & liveness. The closest thing to an owner is crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/stall_guard.rs (StallGuard/StallGuardWriter + the owner-decided 30s TRANSFER_STALL_TIMEOUT), but it deliberately covers only raw-TCP data-plane progress, and at least four other strata each grew their own local policy: blit-daemon's net_timeout::within helper, blit-app's client.rs CONNECT_TIMEOUT, the TUI's SUBSCRIBE_OPEN/SNAPSHOT_FETCH constants, and the prometheus bridge's 5/8/10s set — while the two blit-core channel builders carry bare inline 30s literals. The smear is asymmetric in a dangerous way: connects and daemon-side accepts are well bounded (30s/15s, declared four separate times), but once connected, every client-side gRPC await in the workspace is structurally unbounded — no client channel sets HTTP/2 keepalive, TCP keepalive, or a per-RPC deadline, so delegation progress (remote.rs:734), push responses (helpers.rs:245), all three pull fallback loops (pull.rs:330/505/790), and `blit jobs watch` by default will wait forever on a silently-dead daemon; only the daemon server protects itself (main.rs:138). The sharpest single problem is that the planned fix — the audit-h3c slice-2 progress watchdog — exists only as comments plus a pass-through helper (grpc_fallback.rs:150), and its own TODOs record that the surrounding error-conversion sites already strip the error chain the watchdog would need for retries to work. Secondary findings: raw TCP data-plane connects are unbounded (pull.rs:1710, data_plane.rs:92) while control-plane connects are triple-bounded; pull_sync.rs redefines its own accept/token constants twice in one file under a comment claiming reuse; and the two set_keepalive(true) calls run at OS-default ~2h timing while comments claim they prevent idle-stream timeouts.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:227:- HEADLINE — no client channel in the workspace configures HTTP/2 keepalive, TCP keepalive, or a per-RPC deadline (verified: the only keepalive is the daemon server, main.rs:138-139; no Endpoint anywhere calls .timeout()/.tcp_keepalive()/.http2_keep_alive_interval()). Consequence: every long-lived client-side gRPC await — delegation progress (blit-app/src/transfers/remote.rs:734, :878), push response forwarder (blit-core/src/remote/push/client/helpers.rs:245 feeding mod.rs:439), all three pull fallback receive loops (blit-core/src/remote/pull.rs:330, :505, :790), RemoteFileStream (pull.rs:1232), `blit jobs watch` at default --timeout-secs=0 (blit-cli/src/jobs.rs:305), and the TUI forwarder (blit-tui/src/main.rs:5717) — hangs forever against a silently-dead daemon (kill -9 survives via FIN, but lid-close/partition/NAT-drop does not). Violates RELIABLE ('fails plainly').
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:228:- audit-h3c slice 2 (the gRPC-fallback progress watchdog) is unimplemented: the TCP path has StallGuard but force-gRPC / fallback transfers have zero stall protection (stall_guard.rs:35-41, grpc_fallback.rs:24). Worse, the TODOs at pull.rs:322-329 and :781-788 record that current map_err sites strip the io::Error chain, so even when the watchdog ships, blit_app::transfers::retry::is_retryable will not classify stalls as retryable until every conversion site is also changed — a cross-file coupling already documented as a future drift trap.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:229:- Daemon-side stalled-but-alive client: pull_sync.rs:307/:341/:798/:966 and push/control.rs:62 await client messages with no deadline. grpc_fallback.rs:68-71 declares these covered by 'HTTP/2 keepalive + cancel-on-disconnect', but keepalive only reaps peers that stop ACKing pings — a live client that simply stops sending (or a hostile slow-drip) pins the handler, its ActiveJobs row, and module resources indefinitely. The symmetric send-side gap also exists: daemon server-streaming tx.send(...) awaits (e.g. pull_sync.rs:580-582) block forever on a flow-control-stalled live client; StallGuardWriter protects only the raw TCP data plane.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:245:  - divergence: The same conceptual operation (client connects out to the daemon) is bounded at 30s on the gRPC control plane but completely unbounded on the raw TCP data plane — a black-holed data-plane port hangs for the OS SYN timeout (60-180s) after the control plane connected in milliseconds. The post-connect token writes (pull.rs:1714, data_plane.rs:128) are also unbounded.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:246:- [DIVERGE] Bounding the Subscribe/GetState RPC open against a stalled daemon (the 'feedback-server-await-timeouts' lesson)
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:258:- `TRANSFER_STALL_TIMEOUT` = 30s (idle, re-armed on progress) (crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/stall_guard.rs:69) — RELIABLE: good — converts infinite stalls into clean TimedOut with explanatory text. FAST/SIMPLE: grpc_fallback.rs:73-80 itself argues hardcoded wall-clock constants violate the adapt-at-runtime principle; the idle (not total) semantics mostly defuse it.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:259:- `GRPC_FALLBACK_CHUNK_BYTES` = 1 MiB (crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/grpc_fallback.rs:102) — FAST: deliberately caps fallback frames below the 16-64 MiB TCP tuning so progress is observable at a 100 KB/s floor — trades peak fallback throughput for observability of a watchdog that does not exist yet.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:262:- `SOURCE_CONNECT_TIMEOUT` = 30s (crates/blit-daemon/src/service/delegated_pull.rs:35) — none functionally, but it is a third stacked 30s bound around an already double-bounded connect — stratum evidence.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:277:- /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/stall_guard.rs:69 — Partial owner: TRANSFER_STALL_TIMEOUT (30s) + StallGuard (AsyncRead, :75) + StallGuardWriter (AsyncWrite, :139). Idle timeout re-armed on progress, owner-decided 30s. Module doc (:35-41) records that gRPC-fallback coverage (audit-h3c slice 2) is PENDING. — The only deliberately designed liveness mechanism in the repo; covers TCP data-plane paths only.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:278:- /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/grpc_fallback.rs:150 — recv_fallback_message chokepoint — pass-through today, slice-2 'dynamic progress watchdog' not implemented. GRPC_FALLBACK_CHUNK_BYTES (1 MiB, :102) caps frame size so a future watchdog can observe cadence. — Doc at :50-71 explicitly names every out-of-scope unbounded await in the workspace — a written confession of the gap.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:280:- /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/remote/pull.rs:330 — UNBOUNDED: pull() gRPC-fallback receive loop via recv_fallback_message — no liveness bound until h3c slice 2. TODO at :322-329: map_err strips the io::Error chain, so a future watchdog TimedOut would not be classified retryable.

docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:312:- /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-tui/src/main.rs:5639 — TUI site: SUBSCRIBE_OPEN_TIMEOUT=30s outer timeout around connect + Subscribe RPC (audit-8, the 'feedback-server-await-timeouts' lesson) at :5645.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:322:No module owns chunk sizing; the decision is smeared across at least six regimes in blit-core and blit-daemon: remote/tuning.rs (static byte-count table, the de-facto network authority), transfer_plan.rs (a second, disagreeing heuristic in the transport-agnostic planner), auto_tune (the nominal owner, whose adaptive warmup branches are entirely dead code), buffer.rs BufferSizer (local copies, the only memory-aware path), grpc_fallback.rs (1 MiB gRPC frame ceiling), and per-site literals (daemon's 64 KiB gRPC streamer, five copies of a 256 KiB hash buffer, fixed 1 MiB receive buffers, a 4 MiB tar pool). TCP-derived chunk_bytes leaks through PlannedPayloads into both gRPC sinks where the audit-h3c clamp renders it inert, while one pre-clamp gRPC path (blit-daemon/src/service/pull.rs:602) never got the 1 MiB contract at all and ships 64 KiB frames. The sharpest problems are the dual disagreeing chunk heuristics whose winner depends on call path, the workspace-wide absence of max_decoding_message_size making the 4 MiB tonic default an undeclared protocol invariant guarded only by scattered 1 MiB literals, and network buffer pools that can authorize multi-GiB allocations with no awareness of available memory while local copies are carefully RAM-capped. blit-cli, blit-app, and blit-tui contain no chunk/buffer decision sites (TUI 'chunks' are ratatui layout); blit-prometheus-bridge has one bounded 1 KiB HTTP-head buffer. Searches run: rg for CONTROL_PLANE_CHUNK_SIZE/GRPC_FALLBACK_CHUNK_BYTES/BufferPool; vec![0u8;N] and with_capacity across all crates; determine_remote_tuning/determine_tuning/analyze_warmup_result/BufferSizer/calculate_buffer_size callers; TAR_BUFFER_SIZE/DEFAULT_BLOCK_SIZE/RECEIVE_CHUNK_SIZE/DEFAULT_PAYLOAD_PREFETCH; max_decoding_message_size/max_encoding_message_size (zero hits); GrpcFallbackSink::new/GrpcServerStreamingSink::new/DataPlaneSession construction sites; chunk/buffer keyword sweeps of proto/blit.proto, blit-cli, blit-app, blit-tui; warmup callers (zero outside auto_tune).
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:326:- Two live, disagreeing chunk-size heuristics: remote/tuning.rs:5-11 (16/32/64 MiB, byte-count keyed) vs transfer_plan.rs:223-228 (16/32 MiB, byte-count-or-dominance keyed). Remote paths override the planner via chunk_bytes_override, but push/client/mod.rs:961-965 falls back to planned.chunk_bytes when its chunk_bytes arg is 0 — the same codebase answers 'what is the chunk size for N bytes' two different ways depending on call path.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:328:- Daemon stream_single_file (blit-daemon/src/service/pull.rs:602) sends gRPC file data in bare 64 KiB frames — written before/independent of the audit-h3c 1 MiB frame contract (grpc_fallback.rs), uses no constant, and is invisible to anyone refactoring GRPC_FALLBACK_CHUNK_BYTES. 16x message overhead on the scan/deprecated-pull path.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:339:  - divergence: tuning.rs has a 64 MiB tier (>=8 GiB) absent from transfer_plan; thresholds differ (512 MiB vs 1 GB); criteria differ (pure byte count vs byte count OR >=50% large-file dominance); auto_tune's third heuristic keys on warmup Gbps and is dead. Which one wins depends on whether chunk_bytes_override is set: remote paths override the planner, but push gRPC fallback with chunk_bytes==0 uses planned.chunk_bytes (push/client/mod.rs:961-965), so one transfer can carry two different chunk_bytes notions.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:344:  - sites: /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/sink.rs:971-974 (GrpcFallbackSink); /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/sink.rs:1142-1145 (GrpcServerStreamingSink); /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/payload.rs:250-251 (dead control-plane path); /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-daemon/src/service/pull.rs:602 (stream_single_file)
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:351:  - divergence: Value agrees (256 KiB) in all five copies, but the daemon copy is a stack array ([0u8; 256*1024]) while checksum.rs uses heap vecs; the daemon also reimplements Blake3 file hashing instead of calling checksum::hash_file.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:359:  - sites: /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/data_plane.rs:15 (CONTROL_PLANE_CHUNK_SIZE); /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/data_plane.rs:546 (RECEIVE_CHUNK_SIZE); /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/grpc_fallback.rs:102 (GRPC_FALLBACK_CHUNK_BYTES); /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/copy/file_copy/resume.rs:16 (DEFAULT_BLOCK_SIZE); /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/tar_stream.rs:37 (TarStreamConfig.chunk_size default)
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:360:  - divergence: All equal 1024*1024 today. Only grpc_fallback.rs documents an intentional pairing (with CONTROL_PLANE_CHUNK_SIZE, 'by design', but via duplicate literal not a reference); the other coincidences are undocumented, so changing any one silently breaks an unstated invariant (e.g. RECEIVE_CHUNK_SIZE matching the gRPC frame ceiling).
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:365:- `GRPC_FALLBACK_CHUNK_BYTES` = 1 MiB (/home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/grpc_fallback.rs:102) — SIMPLE/RELIABLE: well-reasoned (observable cadence at 100 KB/s floor), but it silently relies on tonic's undeclared 4 MiB decode default; no max_decoding_message_size is set anywhere in the workspace, so the safety margin is implicit
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:367:- `chunk_bytes table in determine_remote_tuning` = 16/32/64 MiB by total_bytes; streams 4-32; tcp_buffer 4/8 MiB; prefetch 16/32 (/home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/remote/tuning.rs:5) — SIMPLE: static size-keyed table presented as 'auto-tuning'; the actual warmup/bandwidth adaptation path is dead (warmup_result always None). FAST: no feedback from measured throughput
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:375:- `checksum hash buffer` = 256 KiB (x4 here + stack copy at blit-daemon/src/service/pull.rs:524) (/home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/checksum.rs:148) — SIMPLE: five copy-pasted literals; daemon's stack variant adds a 256 KiB frame on an async-adjacent path
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:388:- /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/transfer_plan.rs:223 — duplicate size decision (planner) — build_plan computes its own chunk_bytes (>1GB or >=50% large-file bytes: 32MiB, else 16MiB) — an independent, disagreeing reimplementation of remote/tuning.rs. Overridable via options.chunk_bytes_override (line 229); remote paths set the override, so the planner heuristic only wins when no override is given (e.g. push gRPC fallback when caller passes chunk_bytes==0).
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:389:- /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/payload.rs:112 — TCP size embedded in transport-agnostic type (seed confirmed) — PlannedPayloads.chunk_bytes carries the TCP-tuned chunk size out of the planner. Consumed by gRPC fallback at push/client/mod.rs:961-965 where the clamp makes it inert.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:390:- /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/payload.rs:250 — consumer/clamp + read buffer — transfer_payloads_via_control_plane: chunk = clamp_fallback_chunk_size(chunk_bytes.max(CONTROL_PLANE_CHUNK_SIZE)); vec![0u8; chunk_size] at line 252. Dead code today (comment at 247: 'No live caller') but pub-reachable.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:391:- /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/data_plane.rs:15 — constant definition — CONTROL_PLANE_CHUNK_SIZE = 1 MiB, used as floor in both gRPC sinks and as GrpcFallbackSink chunk at push/client/mod.rs:870.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:395:- /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/grpc_fallback.rs:102 — constant definition + clamp owner — GRPC_FALLBACK_CHUNK_BYTES = 1 MiB; clamp_fallback_chunk_size (line 110) is the ceiling for all gRPC frames. Doc claims it 'matches CONTROL_PLANE_CHUNK_SIZE by design' but they are separate literals with no compile-time tie.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:396:- /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/sink.rs:971 — consumer/clamp (gRPC push fallback) — GrpcFallbackSink: chunk_size = clamp(self.chunk_bytes.max(1MiB)) -> always exactly 1 MiB today; the plumbed-in TCP chunk_bytes field (line 929) is inert. vec![0u8; chunk_size] read buffer at 994; buffer[..n].to_vec() copies every frame (1012).
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:397:- /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/remote/transfer/sink.rs:1142 — consumer/clamp (gRPC pull fallback) — GrpcServerStreamingSink: identical two-bound clamp; chunk_bytes field (1107) equally inert; vec![0u8; chunk_size] at 1165.
docs/audit/DESIGN_MAP_2026-06-11.md:405:- /home/michael/dev/Blit/crates/blit-core/src/remote/push/client/mod.rs:961 — fallback chunk selection — control_chunk = chunk_bytes==0 ? planned.chunk_bytes : chunk_bytes — the one live site where transfer_plan's own chunk heuristic (not remote/tuning's) can feed a gRPC sink; inert anyway due to the 1 MiB clamp.
